questions.detail: The boomerang shape of the brevis indicates a split tear.

questions.question: Anterolateral Impingement Syndrome of the ankle results in a 'meniscoid mass' (balled up scar) located in:
questions.detail: Anterolateral impingement occurs in the lateral gutter.

questions.question: Os Trigonum Syndrome occurs during extreme ankle flexion ('toe pointing') and is classically associated with which population?
questions.detail: Ballet dancers utilizing the 'pointe' technique suffer from os trigonum impingement.

questions.question: The 'Tennis Leg' injury refers specifically to the rupture of which structure?
questions.detail: Tennis leg is a rupture of the plantaris tendon.

questions.question: A calcaneal tuberosity avulsion with the back of the bone 'ripped off' is an 'Aunt Minnie' for which underlying condition?
questions.detail: Calcaneal tuberosity avulsion is an 'Aunt Minnie' for diabetes.

questions.question: According to the WHO criteria for DEXA, a T-score of -1.5 is categorized as:
questions.detail: WHO criteria define osteopenia as a T-score between -1.0 and -2.5.

questions.question: Regional Migratory Osteoporosis is an idiopathic disorder characterized by joint pain that moves from one joint to another. In which demographic is this more common?
questions.detail: Regional Migratory Osteoporosis is more common in men.

questions.question: On MRI, a 'band-like' fracture line that is T1 dark in a vertebral body with normal marrow signal in the non-deformed portions suggests:
questions.detail: A band-like T1 dark line with normal remaining marrow is key for osteoporotic fractures.

questions.question: Which staging of Osteochondritis Dissecans (OCD) is defined as 'Unstable on Probing' with complete discontinuity of the lesion?
questions.detail: Stage 3 OCD is characterized by complete discontinuity and instability.

questions.question: Where is the most common site for Osteochondritis Dissecans (OCD) in the knee?
choices: Lateral aspect of the medial femoral condyle
Medial aspect of the lateral femoral condyle
questions.detail: The lateral aspect of the medial femoral condyle is the #1 site for OCD in the knee.

questions.question: 'Panner’s Disease' of the capitellum occurs in children of which age range?
questions.detail: Panner's is found in younger children (5–10 years).

questions.question: Which childhood osteochondrosis involves the tarsal navicular and is treated non-surgically?
questions.detail: Kohler disease is a self-limiting condition of the tarsal navicular.

questions.question: Scheuermann’s disease is defined by thoracic kyphosis > 40 degrees and wedging of how many adjacent vertebrae?
questions.detail: Diagnosis requires at least 3 adjacent levels of wedging.

questions.question: In the normal pattern of marrow conversion, which areas convert from red to yellow marrow FIRST?
questions.detail: The epiphyses convert to fatty marrow almost immediately after ossification.

questions.question: Normal red marrow should never be darker than which internal control on a T1-weighted MRI?
questions.detail: Red marrow should never be darker than a normal disk or muscle on T1.

questions.question: A 'Blade of Grass' sign (a lucent leading edge in a long bone) is a radiographic hallmark of:
questions.detail: The 'Blade of Grass' is the leading lytic edge of Paget's disease in long bones.

questions.question: 'Osteoporosis Circumscripta' describes the lytic phase of Paget disease in which location?
questions.detail: Osteolysis circumscripta refers to Paget disease in the skull.

questions.question: A 'Picture Frame Vertebra' in Paget disease is caused by cortical thickening on:
choices: The top and bottom endplates only
The anterior and posterior margins only
All 4 sides of the vertebral cortex
questions.detail: Paget disease causes thickening on all 4 sides of the vertebral cortex.

questions.question: What is the most common malignancy found in the foot and ankle of teens and young adults?
questions.detail: Synovial Sarcoma is the most frequent malignancy in this demographic.

questions.question: A mass in the knee described on MRI as a 'Bowl of Grapes' (multiple fluid-fluid levels) should raise suspicion for:
questions.detail: The 'Bowl of Grapes' appearance (multiple fluid-fluid levels in a soft tissue mass) is associated with Synovial Sarcoma.

questions.question: A 45-year-old male is involved in a high-speed motor vehicle accident. Radiographs of the pelvis show an 'Inverted Y' configuration formed by the intersection of two columns of bone. Which landmark represents the 'Anterior Column'?
questions.detail: The iliopectineal line represents the anterior column, while the ilioischial line represents the posterior column.

questions.question: In the 'Denis 3 Spinal Column Concept,' which structures define the 'Middle Column'?
choices: A. Anterior longitudinal ligament and anterior 2/3 of the vertebral body
B. Posterior longitudinal ligament and posterior 1/3 of the vertebral body
D. Spinous processes and interspinous ligaments
questions.detail: The middle column in the Denis system is defined by the PLL and the posterior 1/3 of the vertebral body.

questions.question: A 'Casanova Fracture' refers to bilateral calcaneal fractures. What is the most critical 'Next Step' in imaging for this patient?
choices: B. MRI of the Achilles tendons
C. Radiographs or CT of the spine (T12–L2)
D. Evaluation for a Lisfranc injury
E. Ultrasound of the peroneal tendons
questions.detail: Bilateral calcaneal fractures result from high-energy axial loading, which is frequently associated with spinal burst fractures at the thoracolumbar junction.

questions.question: An active 14-year-old boy presents with pain at the 'Anterior Inferior Iliac Spine (AIIS).' Which muscle is responsible for an avulsion at this site?
questions.detail: The Rectus Femoris attaches to the AIIS, while the Sartorius attaches to the ASIS.

questions.question: An 'isolated avulsion' of the lesser trochanter in an adult with minor trauma should be clinically managed as which of the following?
choices: C. Pathologic fracture concerning for malignancy
E. Result of excessive internal rotation
questions.detail: Lesser trochanter avulsions in adults without significant trauma are pathognomonic for underlying metastatic disease or primary bone malignancy.

questions.question: Which muscle group attaches to the 'Ischial Tuberosity'?
questions.detail: The hamstring muscles originate from the ischial tuberosity.

questions.question: Regarding hip fracture management, why does the degree of fracture displacement correspond directly with the risk of Avascular Necrosis (AVN)?
choices: A. Displaced fractures cause immediate bone resorption
B. Displacement disrupts the vascular flow from the circumflex femorals
C. Displaced fractures always lead to septic arthritis
E. The degree of displacement is irrelevant to AVN risk
questions.detail: Displaced intracapsular fractures disrupt the medial and lateral circumflex femoral arteries that supply the femoral head.

questions.question: In patients with Ankylosing Spondylitis (Ank Spon) undergoing Total Hip Arthroplasty (THA), why is low-dose prophylactic radiation sometimes administered?
choices: B. To treat occult metastatic disease
C. To reduce the risk of heterotopic ossifications
D. To promote bone-ingrowth in uncemented cups
questions.detail: Patients with Ankylosing Spondylitis are at higher risk for heterotopic ossification following THA, which can be mitigated with radiation.

questions.question: 'Stress Shielding' after a Total Hip Arthroplasty is most likely to cause bone resorption in which location?
choices: B. The greater trochanter or calcar
D. The shaft of the femur
questions.detail: Stress shielding occurs when the prosthesis carries the load, causing resorption of proximal bone in the calcar or greater trochanter region.

questions.question: An MRI of a painful hip replacement shows a large fluid collection with 'shaggy' enhancement and debris, extending from the joint. This is the classic look of:
questions.detail: Particle disease involves massive bone resorption and shaggy synovial debris caused by a macrophage response to prosthetic wear.

questions.question: Which nerve is most commonly stretched and injured during an inferior shoulder dislocation (Luxatio Erecta)?
questions.detail: Axillary nerve injury occurs in approximately 60% of patients with an inferior shoulder dislocation.

questions.question: A 'Honda Sign' on a bone scan (H-shaped uptake in the sacrum) is diagnostic of:
questions.detail: The 'Honda Sign' describes the classic H-shaped tracer uptake pattern seen in sacral insufficiency fractures.

questions.question: Which anatomic landmark on the lateral radiograph of the foot is used to identify a 'Flat' angle, suggesting a calcaneal fracture?
questions.detail: Bohler's angle is used to evaluate calcaneal compression fractures.

questions.question: A Bohler’s angle of less than 20 degrees is concerning for:
questions.detail: A Bohler's angle measurement of less than 20 degrees is highly suggestive of a calcaneal fracture.

questions.question: Which of the following is considered a 'High Risk' fracture of the 5th metatarsal due to its propensity for non-union?
choices: A. Avulsion fracture of the tuberosity
B. Jones fracture (1.5 cm distal to the tuberosity)
C. Stress fracture of the distal shaft
D. Comminuted fracture of the head
E. Growth plate fracture in a child
questions.detail: The Jones fracture occurs in a vascular watershed area, making it high risk for non-union.

questions.question: A patient presents with 'Patella Baja' (a low-riding patella) after a fall. This suggests a rupture of which structure?
questions.detail: Quadriceps tendon rupture results in Patella Baja (low riding), while patellar tendon rupture results in Patella Alta (high riding).

questions.question: 'O’Donoghue’s Unhappy Triad' involves injuries to the ACL, the MCL, and which other structure?
questions.detail: The triad consists of tears to the ACL, MCL, and medial meniscus.

questions.question: A 'Segond Fracture' is a cortical avulsion of the lateral tibial plateau. What is the classic mechanism of injury?
choices: A. External rotation and valgus stress
B. Internal rotation and varus stress
questions.detail: Segond fractures are caused by internal rotation and are 75% associated with ACL tears.

questions.question: Which knee ligament is considered the 'strongest'?
questions.detail: The PCL is considered the strongest ligament in the knee joint.

questions.question: On MRI, 'ACL Mucoid Degeneration' is classically described as having which appearance?
questions.detail: ACL mucoid degeneration presents with a striated 'celery stalk' appearance on T2-weighted images.

questions.question: What is the most common site for a tibial stress fracture in a young athlete?
choices: B. Posterior-medial proximal or distal third (compressive side)
questions.detail: The posterior-medial aspect is the most common site for tibial stress fractures in athletes.

questions.question: Which stress fracture location is 'High Risk' and requires aggressive management (e.g., non-weight bearing or surgery)?
choices: D. Compressive side of the femoral neck
questions.detail: High-risk stress fractures include the tarsal navicular, the tensile side of the femoral neck, and the anterior midshaft of the tibia.

questions.question: A 'Stener Lesion' occurs when which structure gets caught in the torn edges of the Ulnar Collateral Ligament (UCL)?
questions.detail: A Stener lesion forms when the adductor aponeurosis is trapped within the torn thumb UCL, preventing healing.

questions.question: Why are 'stress views' of the thumb MCP joint contraindicated if a Stener lesion is suspected?
choices: A. They are diagnostic only in children
B. They can convert a non-displaced tear into a Stener lesion
D. They are less sensitive than ultrasound
questions.detail: Stress views can cause the adductor aponeurosis to get caught between the torn ends of the UCL, creating a Stener lesion.

questions.question: 'Trigger Finger' is the result of scarring and thickening of which pulley system in the hand?
questions.detail: Trigger finger is caused by stenosing tenosynovitis at the A1 pulley.

questions.question: Which imaging modality is most effective for demonstrating the 'thickened sheath' in Trigger Finger?
questions.detail: Ultrasound is the primary imaging modality used to diagnose trigger finger.

questions.question: In the 'Pretzel Principle' of forearm injuries, if a radiologist identifies a fracture of the distal radius, what must they evaluate for at the wrist?
choices: B. Dislocation of the Distal Radial Ulnar Joint (DRUJ)
questions.detail: A distal radial fracture with associated DRUJ dislocation is known as a Galeazzi fracture.

questions.question: A 'Galeazzi Fracture' involves a distal radial shaft fracture and:
questions.detail: Galeazzi = distal Radius fracture + DRUJ (ulnar head) dislocation.

questions.question: A 'Monteggia Fracture' involves a proximal ulnar fracture and:
questions.detail: Monteggia = proximal Ulna fracture + Radial head dislocation.

questions.question: An 'Avian Spur' (supracondylar spur) of the humerus points in which direction?
choices: E. It does not have a consistent direction
questions.detail: Unlike osteochondromas, supracondylar (avian) spurs point toward the joint.

questions.question: Which nerve can be compressed by the 'Ligament of Struthers' in the presence of a supracondylar spur?
questions.detail: The median nerve is compressed within the space formed by the spur and the Ligament of Struthers.

questions.question: 'Lateral Epicondylitis' (Tennis Elbow) involves repetitive injury to which tendon?
questions.detail: Lateral epicondylitis classically involves the ECRB tendon.

questions.question: 'Medial Epicondylitis' (Golfer's Elbow) involves repetitive injury to which tendon?
questions.detail: Medial epicondylitis involves the common flexor tendon.

questions.question: On MRI of the elbow, the 'T-sign' with contrast extending medial to the sublime tubercle indicates a partial tear of which ligament?
questions.detail: The 'T-sign' represents contrast extending medial to the sublime tubercle, diagnostic of a partial UCL tear.

questions.question: Which component of the UCL is the 'most important' for elbow stability in throwers?
questions.detail: The anterior bundle of the UCL is the critical stabilizer for the elbow during the throwing motion.

questions.question: 'Little Leaguer’s Elbow' involves a triad of injuries. Which of the following is NOT part of that triad?
questions.detail: The classic triad is Medial tension, Lateral compression, and Posterior shear. Biceps rupture is not part of the syndrome.

questions.question: 'Dialysis Elbow' is the result of:
choices: B. Chronic olecranon bursitis from pressure during treatment
C. Avulsion of the triceps tendon
D. Amyloid deposition in the joint
questions.detail: Dialysis elbow refers to chronic olecranon bursitis caused by repetitive pressure on the elbow during dialysis.

questions.question: A 'Reverse Hill-Sachs' lesion (trough sign) is found on the anterior-medial humeral head. This is diagnostic of which injury?
questions.detail: A reverse Hill-Sachs lesion or trough sign is indicative of a posterior shoulder dislocation.

questions.question: The 'Light Bulb Sign' on a shoulder radiograph indicates:
choices: A. The humerus is in extreme external rotation
B. The humerus is locked in internal rotation due to posterior dislocation
C. There is a large joint effusion
D. The patient has a full-thickness rotator cuff tear
E. There is an axillary nerve palsy
questions.detail: The 'light bulb sign' occurs when the humerus is locked in internal rotation after a posterior dislocation.

questions.question: According to the Neer classification of proximal humerus fractures, a 'part' is defined by displacement of greater than:
questions.detail: Neer defines a 'part' as a fragment displaced > 1 cm or angulated > 45 degrees.

questions.question: Which shoulder muscle must be fully functional for a patient to undergo a 'Reverse' Total Shoulder Arthroplasty?
questions.detail: Reverse total shoulder arthroplasty relies on a functioning deltoid to serve as the primary mover.

questions.question: 'Subcoracoid Impingement' typically damages which tendon?
choices: E. Long head of the biceps
questions.detail: Subcoracoid impingement is an anterior process that damages the subscapularis.

questions.question: On MRI, how do you definitively diagnose a 'Full-Thickness' rotator cuff tear?
choices: A. High T2 signal in the tendon only
B. T1-weighted images showing Gadolinium in the subacromial bursa
D. Fatty atrophy of the muscle belly
E. Presence of a paralabral cyst
questions.detail: Visualization of joint fluid or contrast in the subacromial bursa proves a full-thickness defect.

questions.question: 'Adhesive Capsulitis' (Frozen Shoulder) is characterized on sagittal T1 MRI by a loss of fat in which location?
questions.detail: The loss of clean fat within the rotator cuff interval is a classic MRI sign of adhesive capsulitis.

questions.question: A 'Paralabral Cyst' in the spinoglenoid notch will typically cause atrophy of which muscle(s)?
questions.detail: A cyst in the spinoglenoid notch is downstream of the supraspinatus branch, thus causing isolated infraspinatus atrophy.

questions.question: A cyst at the 'Suprascapular Notch' will typically cause atrophy of which muscle(s)?
questions.detail: A cyst at the suprascapular notch affects the suprascapular nerve before it branches, impacting both muscles.

questions.question: 'Quadrilateral Space Syndrome' involves compression of the axillary nerve, classically resulting in atrophy of the:
questions.detail: Axillary nerve compression in the quadrilateral space leads to atrophy of the teres minor and deltoid.

questions.question: What are the borders of the 'Quadrilateral Space'?
choices: A. Teres minor (above), Teres major (below), Humeral neck (lateral), Triceps long head (medial)
B. Teres major (above), Teres minor (below), Scapula (lateral), Biceps (medial)
D. Clavicle (above), First rib (below), Sternum (lateral), Lung (medial)
E. Coracoid (above), Lesser tuberosity (below), Glenoid (lateral), Ribs (medial)
questions.detail: The quadrilateral space is bounded by the teres minor, teres major, humeral neck, and long head of the triceps.

questions.question: 'Parsonage-Turner Syndrome' is an idiopathic plexitis. When should a radiologist suggest this diagnosis?
choices: A. When they see a single muscle with fatty atrophy
B. When they see muscles in two or more nerve distributions affected (e.g., suprascapular and axillary)
C. When there is a history of a heavy fall on the shoulder
D. When there is a large bone tumor present
E. When the patient is a professional baseball pitcher
questions.detail: Involvement of multiple nerve distributions in the shoulder suggests an idiopathic plexitis like Parsonage-Turner.

questions.question: 'Pelvic Congestion Syndrome' is most commonly seen in which patient demographic?
choices: A. Elderly men with prostate issues
D. Infants with congenital heart disease
E. Post-menopausal women on estrogen therapy
questions.detail: Pelvic congestion syndrome is a board-favorite diagnosis for chronic pelvic pain in multiparous women.

questions.question: A radiologist is reviewing a 10-day follow-up radiograph for a suspected occult fracture. If the fracture is healing normally, it should appear:
choices: A. More sclerotic due to early callus
B. More lucent due to bone resorption by granulation tissue
C. Completely invisible due to fiber bridging
D. Identical to the initial study
E. Displaced, even if initially non-displaced
questions.detail: Bone resorption at 7–14 days increases lucency, making occult fracture lines visible.

questions.question: Which of the following best defines a 'Pathologic Fracture' for the purpose of a board examination?
choices: A. Any fracture occurring in an elderly patient
B. A fracture associated with an open soft tissue wound
C. A fracture occurring through a pre-existing lytic bone lesion (e.g., metastasis or bone cyst)
D. A stress fracture in a patient with osteoporosis
E. A fracture resulting from a high-energy motor vehicle accident
questions.detail: Pathologic fractures occur specifically through abnormal, diseased bone.

questions.question: A 'Tuft Fracture' of the fingertip with an associated nail bed injury is medically categorized as which of the following?
questions.detail: Tuft fractures with associated nail bed damage are considered open fractures.

questions.question: In a Bennett fracture, which muscle or tendon is responsible for the characteristic dorsolateral dislocation of the metacarpal shaft?
questions.detail: The pull of the APL tendon causes the displacement of the metacarpal shaft in a Bennett fracture.

questions.question: What is the primary radiographic feature that distinguishes a Rolando fracture from a Bennett fracture?
choices: C. Rolando is comminuted (often Y or T shaped)
D. Rolando does not involve the APL tendon
E. Rolando occurs in children only
questions.detail: Rolando fractures are comminuted, whereas Bennett fractures are simple intra-articular fractures.

questions.question: A 'Stener Lesion' is a complication of a Gamekeeper’s Thumb where the ulnar collateral ligament (UCL) becomes trapped by which structure?
choices: A. The flexor pollicis longus tendon
B. The extensor pollicis brevis aponeurosis
questions.detail: The Stener lesion prevents UCL healing due to entrapment by the adductor aponeurosis.

questions.question: On MRI, a Stener lesion is classically described as having which appearance at the base of the proximal phalanx?
questions.detail: The 'yo-yo' appearance on MRI represents the balled-up, retracted UCL.

questions.question: 'Trigger Finger' is the clinical term for stenosing tenosynovitis. Which imaging modality is most high-yield for demonstrating the associated tendon sheath scarring?
questions.detail: Ultrasound is the most effective modality for visualizing the pulley and sheath scarring in trigger finger.

questions.question: According to the 'Pretzel Principle' of forearm fractures, if an isolated fracture of the distal radius is identified, which joint must be carefully imaged to exclude dislocation?
choices: C. The wrist (Distal Radioulnar Joint / DRUJ)
questions.detail: Check the DRUJ whenever an isolated radial fracture is seen (Galeazzi principle).

questions.question: Which anatomic structure is the primary target of damage in Subacromial Impingement?
choices: D. Long head of the biceps
questions.detail: Subacromial impingement most commonly damages the supraspinatus tendon.

questions.question: Subcoracoid Impingement results in 'pinching' of which structure between the lesser tuberosity and the coracoid process?
questions.detail: The subscapularis tendon is pinched in subcoracoid impingement.

questions.question: 'Internal' shoulder impingement (Posterior Superior) is a classic 'buzzword' diagnosis for which patient population?
choices: C. Athletes who perform frequent overhead movements (e.g., pitchers)
questions.detail: Internal impingement is a pathology of overhead athletes resulting in damage to the posterior-superior cuff.

questions.question: On an MRI arthrogram of the shoulder, the presence of Gadolinium within the subacromial bursa is diagnostic of:
choices: A. A partial thickness bursal-surface tear
B. A partial thickness articular-surface tear
C. A full thickness rotator cuff tear
questions.detail: Communication between the joint space and the subacromial bursa indicates a full-thickness defect.

questions.question: A tear of the 'Rotator Cuff Interval' involves the junction between which two tendons?
choices: C. Supraspinatus (anterior fibers) and Subscapularis (superior fibers)
E. Long and short heads of the biceps
questions.detail: The rotator cuff interval is the space between the supraspinatus and subscapularis tendons.

questions.question: Which clinical history is the most common 'multiple choice key' for a diagnosis of Adhesive Capsulitis (Frozen Shoulder)?
questions.detail: A history of prior shoulder trauma or surgery is the classic board trigger for adhesive capsulitis.

questions.question: A 'buzzword' finding for Adhesive Capsulitis during a shoulder joint injection is:
choices: C. Rapid contrast extravasation into the bursa
E. Filling of the subcoracoid bursa
questions.detail: Adhesive capsulitis leads to joint capsule thickening and a dramatic decrease in joint volume.

questions.question: A Segond fracture is a cortical avulsion of the lateral tibial plateau. It is 75% associated with a tear of which ligament?
questions.detail: Segond fractures are lateral and highly associated with ACL tears.

questions.question: A Reverse Segond fracture involves the medial tibial plateau and is associated with which mechanism?
questions.detail: Reverse Segond is medial and associated with external rotation and PCL injury.

questions.question: An 'Arcuate Sign' (avulsion of the proximal fibula) is 90% specific for an injury to which ligament(s)?
questions.detail: Proximal fibular avulsion is highly specific for PCL or arcuate complex injury.

questions.question: The 'Deep Intercondylar Notch Sign' seen on a lateral radiograph is caused by:
choices: A. Chronic friction from a discoid meniscus
B. An impaction injury of the lateral femoral condyle during an ACL tear
D. Patellar maltracking in a dancer
questions.detail: The deep notch results from the impaction of the lateral femur into the tibia during an ACL tear.

questions.question: Why are the ACL and PCL usually unable to heal on their own after a complete tear?
choices: A. They have no blood supply
B. They are composed of type II collagen only
C. They are extrasynovial but intra-articular (the synovium prevents clot formation)
D. They are under constant high tension
E. They are effectively tendons, not ligaments
questions.detail: Synovial fluid prevents stable clot formation, isolating these ligaments from the body's natural healing process.

questions.question: On short TE MRI sequences (T1, PD, GRE), the PCL and Patellar tendon may show intermediate signal when they form a 55-degree angle with the magnetic field. This is called:
questions.detail: The Magic Angle phenomenon is seen at 55 degrees on short TE sequences.

questions.question: A 'Kissing Contusion Pattern' on MRI (lateral femoral condyle sulcus terminalis and posterior lateral tibial plateau) is 95% specific for:
questions.detail: This impaction pattern is pathognomonic for an acute ACL tear.

questions.question: A T2-weighted MRI of the knee shows a striated 'celery stalk' appearance of the ACL in a patient without acute trauma. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.detail: ACL Mucoid Degeneration is characterized by the striated 'celery stalk' appearance.

questions.question: When evaluating an ACL repair graft on MRI, a graft that is 'Too Steep' (more vertical than Blumensaat’s line) is prone to which complication?
choices: B. Impingement by the femur on extension
questions.detail: A graft that is too steep will be impinged by the intercondylar roof during knee extension.

questions.question: At what time interval following ACL reconstruction is the graft at its weakest point during the remodeling process?
questions.detail: The ACL graft is weakest at 4–8 months post-operatively during the remodeling phase.

questions.question: A focal form of post-operative arthrofibrosis that presents as a low-signal mass-like scar in Hoffa’s fat pad and causes a 'palpable audible clunk' is a:
questions.detail: Cyclops lesions are focal arthrofibrosis scars that limit extension after ACL repair.

questions.question: If a radiologist identifies a PCL tear or a posterior knee dislocation, what is the most critical next imaging step if vascular compromise is suspected?
choices: A. Knee CT for occult fractures
B. CTA (CT Angiogram) to evaluate the popliteal artery
E. Ultrasound of the saphenous vein
questions.detail: Check the popliteal flow void to rule out popliteal artery injury following PCL tear or dislocation.

questions.question: Regarding normal meniscal anatomy, which statement is correct?
choices: A. The lateral meniscus is thicker posteriorly
B. The medial meniscus is thicker posteriorly
C. The medial meniscus has equal thickness anteriorly and posteriorly
D. The lateral meniscus is thicker anteriorly
questions.detail: The medial meniscus is normally thicker posteriorly, while the lateral meniscus is of roughly equal thickness.

questions.question: Which part of the meniscus is vascularized (via the geniculate arteries) and has the potential to heal?
choices: E. The meniscus is entirely avascular in adults
questions.detail: Blood supply to the meniscus comes from the periphery (the red zone).

questions.question: On sagittal MRI (3mm thick/1mm gap), a 'Discoid Meniscus' is diagnosed when you identify how many 'bow tie' shapes?
questions.detail: A normal meniscus shows 2 'bow ties'; identifies 3 or more is diagnostic of a discoid meniscus.

questions.question: To diagnose a discoid meniscus on midline coronal MRI, the transverse length of the meniscus must measure:
questions.detail: A transverse length > 15 mm on a midline coronal slice is diagnostic for a discoid meniscus.

questions.question: The 'Double PCL Sign' on MRI is a diagnostic feature of which type of meniscal tear?
choices: E. Fraying of the free edge
questions.detail: A flipped fragment from a bucket handle tear resting anterior to the PCL creates this sign.

questions.question: A patient presents with 'Patella Alta' (a high-riding patella) after acute trauma. Which underlying condition is most strongly associated with spontaneous patellar tendon tears?
questions.detail: SLE is the classic systemic association for spontaneous patellar tendon tears leading to Patella Alta.

questions.question: A 'Bipartite Patella' is a common normal variant. Where is the accessory ossification center typically located?
questions.detail: The superior-lateral corner is the most frequent location for a bipartite patella.

questions.question: In the 'Classic Flat Foot Progression,' failure typically begins with which structure?
questions.detail: Flat foot progression begins with failure of the posterior tibial tendon (PTT).

questions.question: An MRI showing a 'C-shaped' or 'boomerang-shaped' Peroneus Brevis tendon on axial images is diagnostic of:
questions.detail: The boomerang shape of the brevis indicates a longitudinal split tear.

questions.question: Anterolateral Impingement Syndrome of the ankle results in a 'meniscoid mass' (balled up scar) located in which area?
questions.detail: The 'meniscoid mass' is located in the lateral gutter of the ankle joint.

questions.question: Which foot deformity can predispose a patient to Tarsal Tunnel Syndrome by tightening the retinaculum?
questions.detail: Flat feet (pes planus) can tighten the flexor retinaculum, causing nerve compression.

questions.question: In 'Os Trigonum Syndrome,' the accessory ossicle is said to 'put the smash' on which structure?
questions.detail: The Os Trigonum 'smashes' the FHL tendon during extreme plantar flexion.

questions.question: Where is the most common location for an acute Achilles tendon rupture?
choices: B. 4 cm above the calcaneal insertion
D. Within the gastrocnemius muscle belly
questions.detail: Achilles ruptures typically occur in a vascular watershed area 4 cm proximal to the calcaneus.

questions.question: How can you most easily differentiate an Achilles tendon tear from a Xanthoma on MRI?
choices: A. Tears are always bilateral; Xanthomas are unilateral
B. Tears are usually unilateral; Xanthomas are usually bilateral (associated with high cholesterol)
C. Xanthomas show a fluid-filled gap
D. Tears never show tendon thickening
E. There is no imaging difference
questions.detail: Xanthomas are characteristically bilateral, whereas tears are typically unilateral.

questions.question: A 'Tennis Leg' injury refers to the rupture of the plantaris tendon. Where is the classic fluid collection seen on MRI?
choices: B. Within the Achilles tendon gap
C. Between the soleus and the medial head of the gastrocnemius
questions.detail: Tennis leg involves fluid between the soleus and medial gastrocnemius muscles.

questions.question: In the context of DEXA and FRAX, what does the FRAX tool calculate?
choices: A. Bone density relative to an age-matched control
B. Bone density relative to a young adult
C. 10-year probability of a major osteoporotic fracture
D. The amount of calcium in the vertebral body
E. The rate of bone resorption
questions.detail: FRAX is a tool used to calculate the 10-year risk of significant osteoporotic fractures.

questions.question: Which condition, also known as 'Sudeck Atrophy,' is characterized by severe osteopenia and intra-articular uptake on a 3-phase bone scan?
questions.detail: RSD (Complex Regional Pain Syndrome) is also known as Sudeck Atrophy.

questions.question: For the purpose of board exams, 'Transient Osteoporosis of the Hip' classically involves which patient and location?
choices: A. An elderly man; right hip
B. A female in the 3rd trimester of pregnancy; left hip
C. A young athlete; both hips
E. A post-menopausal woman; left hip
questions.detail: The classic MCQ demographic for transient osteoporosis is 3rd trimester pregnancy in the left hip.

questions.question: On a radiograph, how does Transient Osteoporosis of the Hip differ from Avascular Necrosis (AVN)?
choices: A. Transient Osteoporosis has no signal on MRI
B. Transient Osteoporosis is super lucent (can barely see the head); AVN has patchy sclerosis
C. AVN is always bilateral; Transient Osteoporosis is always unilateral
D. Transient Osteoporosis shows a 'Crescent Sign'
E. There is no difference on radiographs
questions.detail: Transient osteoporosis shows profound lucency, unlike the patchy sclerosis and dark lines of AVN.

questions.question: Regarding capitellum lesions, which of the following is true of Panner’s Disease?
choices: A. It occurs in adolescents (12–16 years)
B. It typically shows intra-articular loose bodies
C. It occurs in younger children (5–10 years) and involves the entire capitellum
D. It is highly unstable on MRI
E. It requires immediate surgical fixation
questions.detail: Panner's involves the entire capitellum in younger children (5–10) and is generally stable.

questions.question: Scheuermann’s disease of the spine is defined by thoracic kyphosis > 40 degrees and wedging of at least how many adjacent vertebrae?
questions.detail: Diagnosis of Scheuermann's requires at least 3 adjacent levels of wedging.

questions.question: Kienbock disease (avascular necrosis of the lunate) is most commonly associated with which radiographic finding?
questions.detail: Negative ulnar variance is testably associated with Kienbock disease.
